title: PivotTables
summary: PivotTables let you summarize, group, and analyze large datasets in seconds by dragging fields into rows, columns, and values without writing a single formula.
syntax: Select data > Insert > PivotTable > drag fields into Rows / Columns / Values / Filters
jobUseCases: Summarize total sales by region and product category for a monthly review deck
Count headcount by department and location for an HR org report
Build a month-over-month revenue trend by dragging dates into Columns
Find top-performing salespeople by sorting a Sum of Sales value field
Create a quick expense breakdown by vendor and cost centre for finance

examples.title: Sales by region
examples.formula: Insert > PivotTable > Region to Rows > Sales to Values (Sum)
examples.explanation: With a table of orders, drag the Region field into Rows and the Sales field into Values. Excel returns one row per region showing total sales (e.g. North 4,52,000; South 3,18,000). Right-click a value > Summarize Values By to switch between Sum, Count, and Average.

examples.title: Two-dimensional summary (Region x Category)
examples.formula: Region to Rows > Category to Columns > Sales to Values (Sum)
examples.explanation: Put Region in Rows and Product Category in Columns to build a matrix. Each cell shows sales for that region-category combination, giving you a cross-tab view that highlights which categories sell best in each region.

examples.title: Group dates by month
examples.formula: Order Date to Rows > right-click a date > Group > Months and Years
examples.explanation: Drag a date field into Rows, then right-click any date and choose Group. Selecting Months and Years rolls daily transactions up into monthly totals so you can show a clean Jan-Dec trend instead of hundreds of individual days.

examples.title: Show values as % of total
examples.formula: Sales to Values > right-click value > Show Values As > % of Grand Total
examples.explanation: Add Sales to Values a second time, then right-click and choose Show Values As > % of Grand Total. Each region now shows its share of total sales (e.g. North = 28%), perfect for a contribution analysis without extra formulas.

commonMistakes: Building the PivotTable on a range with blank header cells or merged cells, which causes the field list to break
Forgetting to refresh (Data > Refresh) after the source data changes, leaving stale numbers in the report
Selecting only part of the data so new rows added later are not included; use an Excel Table (Ctrl+T) as the source instead
Leaving a field in the wrong area (e.g. a numeric ID dropped into Values gets summed instead of counted)
Having duplicate or inconsistent category names (e.g. 'North' vs 'north ') that split what should be one group
practiceTips: Convert your source range to a Table with Ctrl+T first so the PivotTable auto-expands when you add rows.
Double-click any value cell to drill through to the underlying rows that make up that number.
Use the Value Field Settings dialog to rename ugly labels like 'Sum of Sales' to 'Total Sales'.
Add Slicers (PivotTable Analyze > Insert Slicer) for clickable, interview-impressive filters.
